data.title: Ping An Biomedical receives Nasdaq letter of non-compliance
data.description:
• Ping An Biomedical (PASW) received Nasdaq letter notifying the company that the closing bid price for the period from October 10, 2025 to November 20, 2025, did not meet the continued listing requirement to maintain a minimum bid price of $1 per share.
  • The company has a 180-calendar-day period, or until May 25, 2026 to regain compliance.

data.title: Major index ETFs climb while stock index futures remain halted
data.description:
Stock index futures remained halted on Friday after CME Group (CME) paused trading due to a cooling issue at a CyrusOne data center.

However, the major index ETFs, SPY (tracks the S&P 500), QQQ (tracks the Nasdaq 100) and DIA (tracks the Dow Jones Industrial Average), continued to trade. 

SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) was up 0.7%, Invesco QQQ Trust, Series 1 (QQQ) rose 0.8% and SPDR Dow Jones Industrial Average ETF Trust (DIA) rose 0.7%. 

U.S. markets are coming off the Thanksgiving holiday and will operate on a shorter schedule today, closing at 1 p.m. ET for Black Friday.

"Meanwhile in the U.S., both equity and bond markets were closed for Thanksgiving, but the futures markets that were opened showed barely any movement either. So around the time of the European close, futures on the S&P 500 were completely flat, and Treasury futures indicated that yields would only rise by up to a basis point across the curve," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said. 

The economic calendar is light, with mainly the Fed balance sheet slated to land later in the day.

data.title: Belgian PM: EU plan for frozen Russian assets could hinder Ukraine peace deal
data.description:
Belgian Prime Minister Bart De Wever has called the European Union's proposal to use frozen Russian assets to fund Ukraine "fundamentally wrong," amid fears that Belgium would be on the hook if Russia legally challenges the move.

"Hastily moving forward on the proposed reparations loan scheme would have, as collateral damage, that we, as the EU, are effectively preventing reaching an eventual peace deal," De Wever wrote in a letter to European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen.

He noted that "at no moment in history, immobilized sovereign assets have been 're-purposed' during an ongoing war," and such assets have typically been part of after-war reparations.

De Wever instead proposed that the EU jointly borrow €45B on the markets to cover Ukraine's financial needs for next year. "Such an option would, as a matter of fact, come cheaper than other options, in particular the option of a reparations loan, if all risks are factored in."

Most of the frozen Russian assets - around €185B - are held at Belgian securities depository Euroclear.

"In the very probable event Russia is ultimately not officially the losing party, it will, as history has shown in other cases, be legitimately asking for its sovereign assets to be returned," De Wever warned.

He added that he would only agree to the EU's proposal if member states agreed to guarantees for covering any costs arising from Russia potentially reclaiming the assets.

EU leaders will convene in Brussels on Dec. 18-19 to decide on the matter.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: BITF, and more
data.description:
Stock futures inched up in Friday's premarket session, positioning the holiday-shortened week and November close on an upbeat trajectory despite disruptions from a CME outage triggered by cooling failures at CyrusOne data centers that halted futures and options trading across equities, commodities, FX, and bonds.

Biggest stock gainers

  • Bitfarms (BITF) +4% - Crypto-related stocks gained traction on Friday with Bitcoin stabilizing around $91,658 after briefly dropping near $80,000 last Friday, its lowest level since April; peers including Marathon Holdings (MARA), MicroStrategy (MSTR), Riot Platforms (RIOT), Robinhood Markets (HOOD), Coinbase Global (COIN), HIVE Digital Technologies (HIVE) also advanced 1% to 3%.

data.title: Siemens Energy rallies to near record high, edges toward €100 billion market cap
data.description:
Siemens Energy AG’s (OTCPK:SMEGF) stock has surged 13% this week, erasing last Friday’s 10% drop, lifting the company’s market value close to the €100 billion ($116 billion) mark.

“Global electricity demand is rising, especially in markets Siemens Energy is in,” said Nikolas Demeter, an analyst at B Metzler Seel Sohn & Co AG, who upgraded the shares to buy on Monday.

He added that the stock remains appealing due to an attractive valuation and the firm’s strong order backlog, Bloomberg News reported.

The German company, spun off from industrial giant Siemens AG in 2020, was Europe’s best-performing stock in 2024 and advanced 320% on surging demand for data centers. It has climbed another 127% so far this year, and the upward momentum is expected to continue.

Siemens Energy AG’s (OTCPK:SMEGF) U.S.-listed ADRs have surged 147% during the same span.

 

 

The firm is “building tomorrow’s energy infrastructure,” said  analysts led by Gael de-Bray, who also raised their targets this week, the report said.

“In recent months, the correlation between AI stocks and Siemens Energy has been relatively high. Looking at this week, however, the picture has shifted somewhat,” Demeter said. “Siemens Energy has performed significantly better than Nvidia (NVDA).”

data.title: SA Sentiment: Mortgage assumability and portability for easing the housing crisis
data.description:
Interesting dynamics have played out in the housing market since the Federal Reserve began raising interest rates to counter inflation back in 2022. Some of those trends go back even further to the onset of the coronavirus pandemic, when the central bank dropped rates to zero at the same time people were seeking more space and lifestyle changes. The effects have led to an increasingly expensive environment for housing and rent, and triggered an affordability crisis across many markets.

Location, location, location? Higher borrowing costs steepened the price of new construction, while supply is short due to the many real estate investors who have clamored into the sector. Landlords have also raised rents to keep up with the inflationary environment, and many retiring baby boomers are still living in their homes rather than downsizing or moving to retirement communities. However, one of the biggest cost drivers is called the "lock-in effect," where people who locked into ultra-low interest rates before 2022 cannot move between markets or make traditional financial changes, which would increase liquidity and free up supply.

There have been many creative solutions to the current state of affairs, some of which are already common across the globe and are now being entertained by the Trump administration. One of them is mortgage assumability, where new owners of a house "assume" the mortgage terms of the seller (with full coverage of existing equity), and it already happens in the U.S. on a minimal scale with FHA and VA loans. Other touted remedies include mortgage portability, which permits buyers or sellers to simply "port" their existing mortgage terms to a new location, as well as the 50-year mortgages that made waves earlier this month.

Possible in America? According to the latest Wall Street Breakfast poll, Seeking Alpha readers think mortgage assumability and portability are a great way to ease the housing crisis, but not so much with 50-year debt. However, there are challenges to overcome. Land ownership in the U.S. is recorded and held at the local level, complicating efforts of porting a mortgage to a different state, or even to a different county. Another factor that also affects assumability is that the majority of mortgages are securitized and bundled with other sales, making it difficult to alter statements and terms, especially if things aren't mitigated by origination premiums, a higher percentage of variable rates, or larger down payments.

data.title: Santacruz Silver Mining GAAP EPS of $0.05, revenue of $79.99M
data.description:
• Santacruz Silver Mining press release (SCZMF): Q3 GAAP EPS of $0.05.
  • Revenue of $79.99M (+9.1% Y/Y).
  • Cash cost per silver equivalent ounce sold ($/oz) of $28.62, a 28% increase year-over-year.
  • AISC per silver equivalent ounce sold of $35.62, a 30% increase year-over-year.
  • Silver Equivalent Ounces produced of 3,424,817, a 30% increase year-over-year.
  • Compared to Q3 2024, consolidated AISC increased to $35.62/oz from $24.27/oz, largely driven by Bolívar, where the impact of the water inflow and ongoing recovery work pushed AISC to $50.89/oz. The stronger boliviano also elevated USD-equivalent operating costs year-over-year, again reflecting the improved political outlook following the change in government

data.title: EU to assess gatekeeper status after Apple confirms its services meet Digital Markets Act requirements
data.description:
U.S. tech giant Apple (AAPL) has informed the European Commission that its Apple ad and Apple Maps services meet the thresholds set by the Digital Markets Act ((DMA)).

The commission now has 45 working days to decide whether to designate Apple as a "gatekeeper" for any of these services. If designated, Apple (AAPL) will have six months to comply with the DMA’s requirements.

The DMA designates companies with more than 45 million monthly active users and 75 billion euros ($79 billion) in market capitalization as gatekeepers, which provide a core platform service for business users.

The so-called gatekeepers have to adhere to a strict set of rules on moderation of content, allowing fair competition and making it easier for consumers to switch between services.

Apple (AAPL), in a statement to Reuters, said Apple Ads is not a large player in the online advertising services market in the EU, and has minimal share compared to rivals such as Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT), TikTok or X.

Maps has very limited usage in the EU when compared to other mapping services, such as Google Maps and Waze, Apple added.
